perturbation.original_text: Developer agrees that money damages would not be an adequate remedy for unauthorized use or disclosure by it of any Sample Data, and that, in addition to all other remedies, Developer shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for Developer’s unauthorized use or disclosure, without the requirement of posting any bond. In the event that FDI deems it appropriate to initiate any action to enforce the obligations of Developer under this Agreement, and should Developer be held to have breached this Agreement, Developer shall reimburse FDI for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer acknowledges that FDI owns the Sample Data and agrees not to challenge FDI’s ownership of the Data or the Sample Data.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the remedies provision to the ownership section reduces its enforceability because remedies are generally expected to be grouped together, not hidden in the ownership details.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3017(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 3017(a) requires that a complaint (or a contract intending to seek similar remedies) 'specify the type of relief to which the party deems itself entitled' in a clear manner, which is undermined by obfuscating the specific remedies (injunctive relief, specific performance, attorneys' fees) within a section focused on ownership rather than remedies.

Demand for relief. (a) Generally. Except as otherwise provided in subdivision (c) of this section, every complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim, interpleader complaint, and third-party complaint shall contain a demand for the relief to which the pleader deems himself entitled. Relief in the alternative or of several different types may be demanded. Except as provided in section 3215, the court may grant any type of relief within its jurisdiction appropriate to the proof whether or not demanded, imposing such terms as may be just. (b) Declaratory judgment. In an action for a declaratory judgment, the demand for relief in the complaint shall specify the rights and other legal relations on which a declaration is requested and state whether further or consequential relief is or could be claimed and the nature and extent of any such relief which is claimed. (c) Personal injury or wrongful death actions. In an action to recover damages for personal injuries or wrongful death, the complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim, interpleader complaint, and third-party complaint shall contain a prayer for general relief but shall not state the amount of damages to which the pleader deems himself entitled. If the action is brought in the supreme court, the pleading shall also state whether or not the amount of damages sought exceeds the jurisdictional limits of all lower courts which would otherwise have jurisdiction. Provided, however, that a party against whom an action to recover damages for personal injuries or wrongful death is brought, may at any time request a supplemental demand setting forth the total damages to which the pleader deems himself entitled. A supplemental demand shall be provided by the party bringing the action within fifteen days of the request. In the event the supplemental demand is not served within fifteen days, the court, on motion, may order that it be served. A supplemental de
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by moving the remedies provision from a section explicitly outlining remedies to a section addressing ownership of data. This undermines the clarity and enforceability of the remedies, potentially violating CPLR § 3017(a), which requires complaints to 'specify the type of relief to which the party deems itself entitled'. Placing the remedies within the ownership section obfuscates them, making it less clear that the party is entitled to specific remedies like injunctive relief or attorneys' fees. The snippet from NYSenate.gov indicates that SECTION 3017 deals with the 'Demand for relief', and that every complaint should contain a demand for the relief to which the pleader deems himself entitled. Moving the remedies provision to a different section undermines this clarity.

perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of the Sample Data, including, without limitation, using the highest degree of care that Developer uses to protect its own confidential information. Developer shall notify FDI in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of Sample Data immediately after it becomes aware of such misuse or misappropriation.Frames Data Inc. By: _________________________ Name: Thomas Lamond Title: President [Developer] By: _________________________ Name: _______________________ Title: ________________________
perturbation.explanation: Including the signature block inside section 1(b) makes the document look unfinished and unsigned, and thus unenforceable, because signature blocks must be at the end of the document.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-701 outlines the statute of frauds, requiring certain agreements to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Placing the signature block in the middle of a clause, rather than at the end of the document, creates ambiguity as to whether the entire agreement is intended to be bound by the signatures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by inserting the signature block within the clause, potentially violates the requirement for a clear indication of intent to be bound by the entire agreement, as suggested by the Law Explanation which references NY GOL § 5-701. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the issue of an unfinished and unsigned document, which could undermine its enforceability. The placement of the signature block creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with legal standards that require a clear and unambiguous indication of agreement to the entire contract. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting that signature blocks typically appear at the end of documents, and the comparison of the original and changed text is enough to satisfy a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Non-Use .Developer shall not use any Sample Data for any purpose other than the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Injunctive Relief. Non-Use .Developer shall not use any Sample Data for any purpose other than the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the key 'Non-Use' definition/clause directly within the Injunctive Relief section, the significance of the Non-Use clause is diminished. This relocation implies that it's merely a condition for injunctive relief rather than a fundamental obligation, potentially weakening its enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act § 3602(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UTSA § 3602(a) defines what constitutes misappropriation of trade secrets and the available remedies. By burying the 'Non-Use' obligation within the Injunctive Relief section, it could be argued that a mere breach of the non-use clause is not automatically misappropriation, but only actionable if it meets the criteria for injunctive relief. This weakens the law's intended protection against unauthorized use of trade secrets, since the core prohibition becomes entangled with the specific conditions for an equitable remedy.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the 'Non-Use' clause into the Injunctive Relief section diminishes its significance, implying it's merely a condition for injunctive relief rather than a fundamental obligation. The Law Explanation supports this, arguing that it could be seen as not automatically misappropriation unless it meets criteria for injunctive relief. Given this potential weakening of a core protection against unauthorized use, and the fact that this falls under the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, there is a significant contradiction. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the combined explanations are sufficient to indicate a contradiction.
